query: material that speeds up a chemical reaction without chemically becoming involved

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Function -- Mechanism of Setting
retrieved_docs.content: The setting of GIC is through an acid-base reaction involving the following stages: Dissolution and decomposition: after mixing the powder and liquid, the acid dissolves the surface of the glass particles to release SiO 4 4- , Ca 2+ , Na + , and F - ions. Initial setting: the Ca 2+ released in the aqueous medium reacts with polyacrylic acid to form a 3-dimensional cross-linked structure. At this time, the material has low strength, and it can be carved using sharp instruments. Final setting: the setting reaction continues during the next 24 hours. During this time, SiO 4 4- forms the silica gel. The slow-moving Al 3+ ions enter the aqueous medium and cross-link the polyacrylic acid chains, displacing the Ca 2+ ions. This increases the final strength of the set cement. Maturation: the aluminum and calcium cross-linked polyacrylate chains get hydrated over time by absorbing water from the aqueous medium. This process is known as maturation. [5] [6]

Setting reaction: The base paste containing hydrosilane-terminated molecules reacts with an accelerator paste containing siloxane oligomers with vinyl end groups and a platinum catalyst. Although no by-product is formed, a secondary reaction leads to hydrogen gas production. That is why it is recommended to wait at least 30 minutes rather than have a pitted cast. [19] 4. Advantages: Excellent dimensional stability - impressions can be stored or posted before casting. Elastic recovery Great accuracy Short setting time Good tear resistance Automix available Hydrophilized addition silicone has good compatibility with gypsum The impression can be cast multiple times without jeopardizing the details [20] 5. Disadvantages: Hydrophobic - the impression area must be dry to prevent inaccuracies in the impression [21] The sulfur present in the latex gloves and rubber dam may interfere with the polymerization of the base and catalyst. Hydrogen gas may lead to pitting in the cast.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Setting Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Hybrid ionomers are set by a dual-reaction curing mechanism involving acid-base and polymerization reactions. When the liquid and powder are mixed, the polymerization reaction starts with chemical or light-mediated initiation. This set resin protects the ongoing acid-base setting reaction in the cement matrix from moisture contamination. This phenomenon is known as the "umbrella effect," reducing the early moisture sensitivity of GIC and providing greater initial strength. [25] The slowly proceeding acid-base reaction determines the final strength.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Proteins Enzymes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Every day, trillions upon trillions of chemical reactions occur in our body to make essential metabolic processes occur. Enzymes are proteins that act upon substrate molecules and decrease the activation energy necessary for a chemical reaction to occur by stabilizing the transition state. This stabilization speeds up reaction rates and makes them happen at physiologically significant rates. Enzymes bind substrates at key locations in their structure called active sites. They are typically highly specific and only bind certain substrates for certain reactions. Without enzymes, most metabolic reactions would take much longer and would not be fast enough to sustain life.

retrieved_docs.title: Diborane Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Diborane's reactivity arises from its structural instability, having 2 hydrogen atoms forming 2 covalent bonds in a highly sterically strained ring with 2 sp3-hybridized boron atoms (see Image . Diborane Structure). [3] Diborane is used as a rocket propellant, reducing agent, rubber vulcanizer, electronic semiconductor dopant, hydrogen polymerization catalyst, and flame-speed accelerator. [4] Cases of toxic diborane exposure are very rare due to the chemical's limited uses and handling difficulties.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Peptide -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Each amino acid comprising a peptide is called a “residue” since that is the portion remaining after the loss of water in the dehydration reaction. Amino acids are the organic starting molecule composed of a carboxyl-terminal and an amino group that makes up the foundation of a protein. Peptide synthesis depends on three main reactions: 1. an amino acid goes through a deprotection step, a preparatory reaction that adds the next amino acid to the chain, and lastly, a coupling reaction that forms the final peptide with functionality. [1] In the second step, the amino acid becomes activated with several reagents. Thes carboxylic acid in the amino acid will react to make the activated form, which will then enter into a coupling reaction. After one round of peptide synthesis, this process is repeatable to add more amino acids until creating the desired length of the peptide.

The second stage begins after all the trisodium phosphate has been consumed, providing the optimum working time for manipulating alginate. In the gelation reaction, potassium alginate reacts with calcium sulfate to form calcium alginate gel. 6. Disadvantages: Low tear strength Dimensional instability 8. Modified alginates:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Setting Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The retarder can slow the setting reaction, giving rise to dental alginates with different working times. Type I is a fast set that hardens within 1 to 2 minutes, and type II is a standard set that hardens within 2 to 5 minutes. [3] The setting reaction time of dental alginates also depends on the water temperature used for the mixture. Warmer water speeds up the setting reaction, and colder water slows the reaction down. [3] The water-to-powder ratio also affects the setting reaction; the hardening reaction is faster with more powder. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Dustless alginates: manufactured to prevent inhalation of alginate particles, which may cause respiratory problems. The alginate particles are coated with glycol or glycerine to make them denser. Chromatic alginates: change colors during manipulation to guide mixing time and setting time. In the form of sol or paste (containing water) and a reactor (plaster of Paris) separately. Agar Agar is another hydrophilic colloid impression material like alginate, but it is reversible. It is the first elastic impression material to be used in dentistry. 1. Composition: Chemically, agar is a sulfuric ester of the galactan complex, and agar is available as a tray or syringe material. Agar (gelling agent) Borax (strengthener) Potassium sulfate (gypsum hardener) Alkyl benzoate (preservatives) Water (reaction medium > 80%) Fillers, flavoring, and coloring agents

retrieved_docs.title: Electrochemiluminescence Method -- Introduction -- Ion Annihilation Electrochemiluminescence System
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of electrochemiluminescence systems currently in use are co-reactant systems. [6] These systems use a high-efficiency co-reactant added to the luminophore with one-directional potential scanning. Oxidation or reduction of both species at the electrode generates radicals. Intermediates from the co-reactant decompose, forming a robust species that reacts with the luminophore, producing excited states and emitting light. Co-reactant systems are used for biomolecular assays due to the solubility of the co-reactant in the surrounding medium, low reduction-oxidation potential, and stability. [7] Ruthenium metal ions and luminol derivatives are the most commonly used lumiphores in co-reactant electrochemiluminescence systems. [8] The co-reactant commonly used with ruthenium metal ions is tripropylamine. Other widely used co-reactants include 2-(dibutylamino)ethanol, peroxydisulfate, and hydrogen peroxide. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, the solid-state reaction yields a stoichiometric and well-defined crystalline structure of hydroxyapatite. However, this method requires high temperatures and extended heat treatment procedures. The raw materials used in the solid-state method should have a Ca/P ratio of 1.67 and must be ball-milled to ensure uniform particle size. [2] The solid-state reaction method solely depends on the solid diffusion of ions into the raw materials, thereby necessitating temperatures around 1250 ºC to initiate the reaction. [2] Moreover, prolonged heat treatment transforms singular crystalline particles into more blocky crystals, and the increase in crystalline size leads to a decrease in porosity, which is associated with the aging process. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Biotransformation -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Reactions classify as Phase I, Phase II, or Phase III. It is important to note that these reactions do not have to take place sequentially and can even take place in reverse, Phase II, and then Phase I, or as a single reaction. Phase I metabolism consists of reduction, oxidation, or hydrolysis reactions. These reactions serve to convert lipophilic drugs into more polar molecules by adding or exposing a polar functional group such as -NH2 or -OH. These reactions also often create active metabolites and which is beneficial in activating prodrugs into their active and therapeutic state. Phase I includes the use of the cytochrome P450 system found with the membrane of the endoplasmic reticulum. If the CYP450 system becomes inhibited in any way the drug level will increase, and vice versa if induced, the drug level will decrease. Phase I reaction using the CYP450 system includes both an oxidative and reductive step using NADPH and not ATP: Drug + O2+ NADPH -> Drug*+ H20 + NADP+ .

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Proteins Enzymes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The initial step occurs when an enzyme binds to a substrate to form an enzyme-substrate [ES] complex (reaction 1). Increasing the concentration of a substrate [S] will, in turn, increase the rate of reaction until it reaches maximum velocity.  After forming the ES, a product forms that dissociates from the enzyme, and the enzyme is then ready to repeat the catalysis steps.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Polymerase Chain Reaction -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The primers, added to the solution in excess, bind to the beginning of the 3' end of each template strand and prevent re-hybridization of the template strand with itself. Lastly, enzyme-driven DNA replication begins by setting the reaction temperature to the point that optimizes DNA polymerase enzyme activity, around 75 to 80°C. At this point, DNA polymerase, which needs double-stranded DNA to begin replication, synthesizes a new DNA strand by assembling free nucleotides in solution in the 5' to 3' direction to produce two complete sets of complementary strands. The newly synthesized DNA is now identical to the template and will be used as such in the progression of PCR cycles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1296
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorbutanol and calcium phosphate are both classified as industrial aids, meaning each serves as a chemical agent used to facilitate various industrial processes. As types of industrial aid, these substances are employed to improve efficiency, act as catalysts, or modify material properties in manufacturing contexts, highlighting their functional role in industry rather than being primary end‑products.

Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how specific active molecules, their parent or precursor compounds, and formulation excipients are organized within pharmacological classes and therapeutic products, providing a comprehensive view of their chemical taxonomy, mechanisms of action, and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium phosphate functions as an industrial aid, meaning it is employed in manufacturing and processing contexts to support or improve industrial operations. The concept of industrial aid therefore includes substances such as calcium phosphate that assist in various industrial processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2015
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric hydroxide and calcium phosphate are both categorized as types of industrial aid, indicating that they are substances used to support and facilitate various industrial processes. As forms of industrial aid, ferric hydroxide and calcium phosphate serve functional roles such as acting as additives, precipitants, or raw materials that enhance manufacturing, treatment, or production operations. Their classification under the broader concept of industrial aid underscores their importance in improving efficiency and outcomes in industrial settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 85
retrieved_docs.content: the pharmacologically active component is anhydrous dextrose (the active moiety), while dextrose monohydrate is listed as the active ingredient; together these anhydrous and monohydrate forms constitute the essential active substance of the formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1616
retrieved_docs.content: Guaietolin and ring compounds are both classified as heterocyclic compounds, a category of organic molecules that contain ring structures incorporating heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen, or sulfur. Recognizing guaietolin as a heterocyclic compound highlights its specific chemical behavior and potential pharmacological properties, while the broader class of ring compounds includes many heterocyclic structures that are important in medicinal chemistry. Understanding that both guaietolin and generic ring compounds belong to the heterocyclic class aids clinicians and researchers in predicting reactivity, planning synthesis pathways, and exploring therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2527
retrieved_docs.content: Isopropamide iodide is categorized as a bulk compounding chemical, and bulk compounding chemicals are classified within the broader chemical group of tricalcium phosphate, linking the specific compound to its larger chemical family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1945
retrieved_docs.content: Phenyl salicylate is classified as a carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product, indicating that it belongs to the broader chemical class of substances that incorporate a carboxyl functional group or its derivatives. This relationship underscores the chemical nature of phenyl salicylate and situates it within the larger family of carboxylic acid‑related compounds, which is relevant for understanding its pharmacological properties, formulation considerations, and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals are a class of substances used in pharmaceutical preparation, and within this class calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are identified as specific members. Calcium compounds represent another category of calcium‑based substances, comprising calcium acetate, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate. Consequently, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are recognized both as bulk compounding chemicals and as calcium compounds, highlighting their dual role in formulation processes and calcium chemistry. This overlap underscores the importance of these agents in both compounding practices and calcium‑related therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1409
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium citrate is a chemical compound that is derived from citric acid, with citric acid serving as its parent molecule.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals represent a category of pharmaceutical excipients that includes several inorganic substances. Within this group, železo (iron) is recognized as a related component, and the category also comprises calcium oxide and calcium phosphate dibasic as specific bulk compounding agents. Additionally, chelátory železa (iron chelators) are identified as derivatives that have iron (železo) as their parent compound, linking the chelating agents directly to the iron element within the broader context of bulk compounding chemicals. This interconnected framework highlights how iron, calcium-based compounds, and their respective chelators are organized under the umbrella of bulk compounding chemicals, informing formulation strategies and material selection in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.
